text: The solute carrier organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATP) family members are responsible for the transport of a wide variety of amphipathic organic anions, including bile salts and steroids, as well as many drugs such as statins and chemotherapeutic agents. OATP1B1 is one of the key proteins responsible for drug clearance in the liver and is thus included in the guidelines of the FDA and EMA for in vitro drug-drug interaction studies. Although physiologically and clinically important, little is known about the 3D structure of the OATPs and how the substrates are transported. Here, we present cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 in different substrate-bound conformations and show the binding of physiological substrates such as bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate as well as the chemotherapeutic drug simeprevir and the fluorescent dye 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein in both the outward- and inward-open states. Altogether, they reveal the location of the main and accessory substrate-binding sites, the dynamics of the movements between the O-state and I-state and elucidate the mechanism of OATP1B1-substrate interaction through the integration of functional data obtained from point mutations, and all-atom MD simulations, and provide new knowledge for understanding the function of the OATP family members and the design of models for predicting potential drug-drug interactions.
